perturbation.original_text: Within thirty (30) days after delivery of such notification, the Indemnifying Party may, upon written notice thereof to the Indemnified Party, assume control of the defense of such Claim with counsel reasonably satisfactory to the Indemnified Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Within ninety (90) days after delivery of such notification, the Indemnifying Party may, upon written notice thereof to the Indemnified Party, assume control of the defense of such Claim with counsel reasonably satisfactory to the Indemnified Party.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the period for the Indemnifying Party to assume control of the defense from 30 to 90 days may conflict with state laws or procedural rules that require a more prompt response to legal claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(a)(1)(A)(i) requires a defendant to serve an answer within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint. While the contract deals with indemnification *between* parties, lengthening the period to 90 days before potentially assuming control of a defense significantly delays the overall response to a claim and could prejudice the indemnified party's ability to mount a timely and effective defense. This contractual term could prevent the indemnified party from meeting their obligation to respond to a complaint in the time afforded by law, especially if the indemnifying party refuses to assume the case. Moreover, some states may have statutes requiring that insurance companies resolve claims within specific deadlines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing (a) Time to Serve a Responsive Pleading. Unless another time is specified by a federal statute, the time for serving a responsive pleading is as follows: (1) In General. (A) A defendant must serve an answer: (i) within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint; or (ii) if it has timely waived service under Rule 4(d) , within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. (B) A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim or crossclaim within 21 days after being served with the pleading that states the counterclaim or crossclaim. (C) A party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply, unless the order specifies a different time. (2) United States and Its Agencies, Officers, or Employees Sued in an Official Capacity. The United States, a United States agency, or a United States officer or employee sued only in an official capacity must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the United States attorney. (3) United States Officers or Employees Sued in an Individual Capacity. A United States officer or employee sued in an individual capacity for an act or omission occurring in connection with duties performed on the United States’ behalf must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the officer or employee or service on the United States attorney, whichever is later. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the timeframe for the Indemnifying Party to assume control of the defense from 30 to 90 days. This contradicts Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(a)(1)(A)(i), which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires a defendant to serve an answer within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint. While the contract addresses indemnification between parties, delaying the response to 90 days before potentially assuming control of a defense significantly delays the overall response to a claim, which could prejudice the indemnified party's ability to mount a timely and effective defense and may violate Rule 12(a)(1)(A)(i).

perturbation.original_text: Exact shall pay the invoice provided by Pfizer within five (5) days of the date that it receives the invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Exact shall pay the invoice provided by Pfizer within sixty (60) days of the date that it receives the invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the payment term to 60 days could conflict with state prompt payment laws, which often mandate shorter payment periods for certain types of services.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Business and Commerce Code § 28.004 requires that payment be made not later than the 30th day after the date the bill is received. By extending the payment deadline to 60 days, the modified contract violates the Texas prompt payment law, making the term unenforceable in Texas, should the contract be interpreted to fall under this statute.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment deadline to 60 days, while the Law Explanation indicates that Texas Business and Commerce Code § 28.004 requires payment within 30 days. This extension directly contradicts the prompt payment law, thus creating a legal contradiction by setting a deadline that violates state law, falling under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction. The absence of scraped snippets doesn't negate the LLM's assertion that the contract violates Texas prompt payment law.
